english: Chinese herbal jelly with sugar syrup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a grass jelly entry (FOODON_03311737); the syrup-added variant is not separately coded. Closest parent match used as the base ingredient is grass jelly; syrup addition documented in notes.
ontology.foodex2_description: Grass jelly (Xiancao) — FoodEx2 composite dessert category; closest defensible code for herbal jelly preparations in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for herbal jelly with syrup as a combined product. A0CKP (jelly-type dessert) used as closest parent match. Syrup addition is a preparation modifier not separately encoded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for herbal jelly or grass jelly dessert as of 2026.

main_ingredients: starch (tapioca or rice starch as gelling agent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of herbal jelly with syrup is approximately 200 g (one bowl), as commonly served at Singaporean hawker stalls and dessert shops.

nutrient_source.title: Herbal Jelly with Syrup — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F02030007 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this exact HPB entry. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this item and is set to 0.0 as confirmed negligible for a plant-based herbal jelly; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat are set to -1 as not reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on grass jelly / sugar-sweetened jelly dessert category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for herbal jelly with syrup (xiancao with sugar syrup) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 51 (low-medium boundary) based on: (1) grass jelly base has a very low GI due to high water content and minimal starch; (2) added sugar syrup (sucrose) has GI ~65; (3) the combined product at 7.34g carbohydrate/100g with 6.58g sugar is analogous to a lightly sweetened jelly dessert. Analogous reference: sugar-sweetened jelly desserts in the Sydney GI database cluster at GI 50–55. GL per serving = (51 × 7.34 × 2.0) / 100 = 7.5 (200g serving, 14.68g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar (6.58g/100g) with zero dietary fibre and negligible potassium; empty-calorie sugar load without compensating cardiovascular-protective nutrients
hypertension.guidance: Sodium is low at 9mg/100g and does not raise blood pressure concern, but the added sugar syrup provides empty calories with no fibre or potassium to support vascular health; individuals with hypertension should request plain herbal jelly without syrup, which is a genuinely heart-healthy low-calorie option.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar syrup contributing 6.58g sugar/100g with zero dietary fibre to slow absorption, classifying this as a sugar-sweetened dessert unsuitable for blood glucose management
type2_diabetes.guidance: The 6.58g/100g added sugar with no fibre buffer triggers rapid glycaemic response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this preparation and instead choose plain herbal jelly without syrup, which is low-calorie and low-GI and does not raise blood glucose significantly.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fat is negligible (0g/100g) and sodium is low (9mg/100g), which are positive factors, but added sugar (6.58g/100g) with no fibre or potassium represents an empty-calorie sugar load that contributes to dyslipidaemia and metabolic risk when consumed regularly
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the fat and sodium profile is favourable for cardiovascular health, the added sugar syrup provides no protective nutrients; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose plain herbal jelly without syrup to obtain the low-calorie, low-fat benefit without the added sugar burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (6.58g/100g) with zero fibre promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; the herbal jelly base (xiancao) has traditional anti-inflammatory properties but these are negated by the sugar syrup addition
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Plain herbal jelly without syrup would be suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory properties of xiancao herb; the syrup-added version should be avoided or the syrup minimised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Herbal jelly with syrup (仙草冻加糖浆; Xiancao jelly) is a traditional Chinese dessert widely consumed in Singapore, Malaysia, and southern China. It is made by boiling dried grass jelly herb (Platostoma palustre, formerly Mesona chinensis), straining the liquid, mixing with a small amount of starch as a gelling agent, and allowing it to set into a firm, dark-coloured jelly. The jelly is then served chilled and drizzled with sugar syrup or honey. The texture is smooth, slightly firm, and silky, with a mildly bitter, earthy herbal flavour that is balanced by the sweetness of the syrup. Nutritionally, herbal jelly with syrup is a low-calorie dessert at 30 kcal per 100g, with minimal protein (0.13g), negligible fat (0g), and moderate carbohydrate (7.34g) predominantly from the added sugar syrup (6.58g sugar/100g). Sodium is very low at 9mg/100g. Dietary fibre is absent (0g/100g). The food provides a small amount of calcium (11mg/100g) and iron (1.21mg/100g) from the herbal base. Clinically, the syrup addition makes this dessert unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose, as the 6.58g/100g added sugar with zero fibre provides no glycaemic buffering. The plain version without syrup is a genuinely low-GI, low-calorie option suitable for most health conditions. In Singapore hawker culture, herbal jelly is a popular dessert available at dessert stalls and is often consumed as a cooling food in traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) practice, where xiancao is believed to have heat-clearing and detoxifying properties.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F02030007, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and consistent with the food type. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, appropriately documented.
notes: Fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database for this herbal jelly preparation; herbal jelly is a plant-based water-set gel with no added fat, making 0g fat naturally zero by formulation. Saturated_fat_g is set to 0.0 as confirmed negligible for this plant-based product with no fat-containing ingredients. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source; the jelly-making process removes most fibre from the herb. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based food containing no animal products. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this food item as of 2026. Note: the input record description references 'tortoise shell extract' as a traditional ingredient; modern commercial herbal jelly in Singapore is predominantly plant-based (xiancao herb only) and the tortoise shell component is largely absent from contemporary preparations. The HPB nutrient data reflects the commercially available plant-based version. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 51) based on sugar-sweetened jelly dessert analogues from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for xiancao with syrup is available in the database as of 2026. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
